driver.body: Supply is one piston; **demand is the other, and it carries a spatial dimension supply does not** [S-045]. A courier can be re-routed, but a merchant is a fixed point on the map, and the density of merchants and orders inside a given cell is what decides whether batching — the single biggest efficiency lever in the whole logistics network — is even geometrically possible. Thin, spread-out demand cannot be batched: every drop sits too far from the next for one trip to carry two, so it stays expensive to serve at any courier count you throw at it. Dense demand is the opposite — self-lubricating. The same geography that concentrates orders into a tight cell is what lets each courier trip absorb more of them, so density is not a nice-to-have on the buy side; it is the precondition the entire cost structure hangs on.

mechanisms.body:
The mechanism is a threshold, not a slope. **Below a critical order density the routing engine cannot stack drops**, so every order consumes a whole courier trip end to end; **above it, three drops share one trip and effective supply triples without a single extra courier being added** [S-046]. Kepler manages its cities explicitly against that line, sorting cells by where they sit relative to the batching threshold rather than by raw order count.

That non-linearity is the whole strategy [S-315]. The jump from an "approaching" cell to a "liquid" one flips contribution from negative to positive on the strength of the batching multiplier — not on any price change, not on a better courier deal, purely on the geometry crossing the threshold. Because the gain is a step function, the growth playbook inverts: it is not "add cities" but **"concentrate demand within a city until its cells cross the batching threshold, then widen"** — a land-and-densify motion rather than a land-grab [S-316].

This is where the flywheel does real work. Kepler does not have to guess which cells can support density before it spends a euro of merchant acquisition, because it reads D1 mobility data to see exactly which cells already carry the foot traffic to sustain it. Mobility has already mapped the demand geography of all 42 cities, so Logistics launches into pre-validated dense cells instead of guessing — a cross-domain advantage no single-vertical courier network can reproduce [S-046].

When demand spikes past the supply that is actually online, **price is the only fast lever** — surge pulls the elastic casual tail online (Driver 2) and, secondarily, rations demand down toward what the network can clear [S-047]. The supply–demand equilibrium chart is the control surface the surge engine reads before it acts [S-317].

Read it left to right. At the pre-surge point ○, peak demand D_peak sits far to the right of base supply S₀, and S₀ is nearly vertical because the inelastic core of couriers will not add hours for a small bump. So the market cannot clear: **fill collapses to 71%, ETAs blow out, and both the merchant funnel and the eater funnel start leaking within the hour** [S-317]. Surge shifts supply to S₁ by paying the elastic tail enough to come online, moving the clearing point to ★ at **96% fill**.

The engine's `raise_surge` call (Mechanism 3.1) is literally walking this curve — it reads the Driver-2 elasticity table and chooses the smallest multiplier that reaches ★. The two failure modes bound the search: set surge too low and the market stays illiquid; set it too high and you over-pay the inelastic core for hours they would have worked anyway while rationing away demand you actually wanted. The flywheel tilts the whole curve in Kepler's favour. Because the casual tail charges on D2's network and is paid instantly by D4, Kepler can pull that tail online at a lower multiplier than a standalone competitor can — **cheaper surge for equal fill is a direct L advantage**, feeding the L term in the moat identity M = D × B × L × T [S-318].
